title: Ad-Click / Analytics Pipeline — System Design
description: Design a pipeline that ingests billions of ad impression and click events per day and turns them into accurate, queryable campaign metrics. The themes are high-throughput ingestion, stream vs batch processing, deduplication for correct counts, attribution, and the freshness-vs-accuracy tradeoff.

nodes.label: Ad Client / Browser
nodes.description: Emits impression and click events as users interact with ads.

nodes.label: Event Collector (Edge)
nodes.description: Lightweight endpoint that validates, enriches, and accepts events fast.

nodes.label: Event Log (Kafka)
nodes.description: Durable, partitioned, ordered event log buffering all ingested events.

nodes.label: Stream Processor
nodes.description: Real-time consumer: dedup, windowed aggregation, attribution.

nodes.label: Batch Processor
nodes.description: Periodic reprocessing of raw events for accurate, corrected metrics.

nodes.label: Raw Event Store (Data Lake)
nodes.description: Immutable archive of every raw event for replay and batch recompute.

nodes.label: Dedup Store
nodes.description: Seen-event-id set / Bloom filter for exactly-once-ish counting.

nodes.label: Query / Dashboard API
nodes.description: Serves aggregated metrics to dashboards and advertisers.

nodes.label: Dashboard / Advertiser
nodes.description: Views near-real-time and historical campaign metrics.

nodes.label: Attribution Service
nodes.description: Joins clicks to conversions within a window to credit campaigns, using recent-click keyed state on the hot path.

nodes.label: Click-State Store
nodes.description: Keyed, time-windowed state of recent clicks (by user/session/click id) for near-real-time attribution.

nodes.label: DLQ / Quarantine
nodes.description: Holds invalid, malformed, schema-incompatible, or too-late events for inspection and replay after a fix.

functional: Ingest impression and click events at very high throughput.
Produce near-real-time metrics (clicks, CTR) within seconds.
Produce accurate, corrected daily metrics (the billing source of truth).
Attribute conversions to the click/campaign that drove them.
Let advertisers query metrics sliced by campaign, time, geo, etc.
nonFunctional: Durable ingestion: never lose an accepted event (billing depends on it).
Correct counts: dedup retried/duplicate events (no over-counting).
Low query latency on aggregated metrics.
Reprocessable: fix a bug and recompute history from raw events.

capacity.label: Events per second
capacity.note: Impressions dominate (~90%); clicks ~1-2%, conversions far rarer. Bursty around large campaigns.

capacity.label: Events per day
capacity.note: 1M/s x 86,400s. Drives log, raw-store, and batch-runtime sizing.

capacity.label: Event size
capacity.note: event_id, type, advertiser/campaign/creative ids, user/session, two timestamps, geo/device context.

capacity.label: Replicated log storage
capacity.note: 34 TB/day x 3 replicas; ~300-700 TB live log for a 3-7 day replay window.

capacity.label: Raw lake storage
capacity.note: 34 TB/day raw -> ~3x columnar/Parquet compression; retained months-to-years for billing audit.

capacity.label: Kafka partitions
capacity.note: Bandwidth alone is modest: 400 MB/s ÷ ~10-20 MB/s per partition ≈ 20-40 partitions. The driver is event-rate and consumer parallelism: 3M events/s peak ÷ a safe ~10-20k events/s per partition-consumer ≈ 150-300, then ~2-3x peak headroom and per-tenant/hot-key spread → ~500-1,000. Not thousands; thousands would be cargo-culting.

capacity.label: Partition key
capacity.note: campaign_id gives aggregation locality but big campaigns hot-key one partition; salt as campaign_id + bucket (then re-key on aggregate). Attribution needs user/session/click-id locality instead, so the speed layer repartitions by match key (two-stage shuffle) rather than reusing the ingest key.

capacity.label: Dedup state
capacity.note: Distinct event_ids in the dedup window x ~50-100B/entry; e.g. ~1B ids/window x 64B ~= 64 GB keyed state.

capacity.label: Click-state (attribution)
capacity.note: A 7-day attribution window holds every click in keyed state: ~1-2% of 86B events/day are clicks ≈ ~1-1.7B clicks/day x 7d x ~100-200B/entry ≈ ~1-2 TB. This dominates the hot-path state footprint — far larger than the dedup window — so it is sharded by match key and TTL'd at the window edge.

capacity.label: Rollup cardinality
capacity.note: campaign_id (~1M) x time bucket x a few dims explodes fast; only an allowlist of dimensions is pre-aggregated.

capacity.label: Batch correction
capacity.note: Accurate, billing-grade recompute; full-day reprocess must finish well inside the next day.

api.description: Ingest a batch of typed ad events (impression, click, conversion). Accepted fast and durably; processed downstream. Required fields are per type: impressions/clicks must carry campaign/creative/placement; conversions must carry a usable attribution identity (user_id, session_id, or click_id) plus event_id/schema_version — campaign_id on a conversion is optional and not authoritative, because attribution derives the credited campaign by matching the conversion to a recent click. Returns partial-acceptance details so a bad event in the batch doesn't reject the rest.
Query aggregated metrics for a campaign over a time range. Each bucket carries a freshness/source marker so the dashboard can show whether a window is settled (batch) or still provisional (speed).

dataModel.name: raw_event
dataModel.note: Immutable record of one ad event (in the log and data lake). Source of truth for replay and batch recompute.

fields.name: event_id
fields.type: uuid (stable, client-generated; idempotency key for dedup)

fields.name: campaign_id
fields.type: string (partition/aggregation key on impressions/clicks; nullable & non-authoritative on conversions — attribution derives the credited campaign)

fields.name: placement_id
fields.type: string, nullable (where the ad rendered; impressions/clicks)

fields.name: session_id / click_id
fields.type: string, nullable (attribution match keys; click_id links a conversion to a specific click)

fields.name: event_time
fields.type: timestamp (when it happened on the client)

fields.name: ingest_time
fields.type: timestamp (when the collector appended it) — watermarks use event_time, lag = ingest - event

fields.name: schema_version
fields.type: int (lets parsers evolve; mismatches go to quarantine)

dataModel.name: credited_conversion
dataModel.note: Output of attribution: a conversion linked to the click/campaign credited for it. Written explicitly, not implied inside generic OLAP updates.

fields.name: credit
fields.type: decimal (1.0 for single-touch; fractional for multi-touch)

fields.name: source
fields.type: enum(speed, batch) + version (so re-attribution is idempotent)

dataModel.name: metric_rollup
dataModel.note: Pre-aggregated metrics in the OLAP store. Keyed so writes are idempotent and batch can version-overwrite speed without double-counting.

fields.name: source
fields.type: enum(speed, batch) (part of metric_key; query picks per window)

fields.name: version
fields.type: int (monotonic; a higher batch version supersedes earlier rows)

fields.name: settled
fields.type: bool (true once batch has finalized the window)

fields.name: computed_at
fields.type: timestamp (freshness indicator surfaced to the query API)

technologyChoices.concern: Edge event collector, validation, and abuse controls
tradeoff: A thin collector behind an API edge can terminate TLS, shape traffic, validate required fields, stamp consent context, and keep the hot path stateless.
The collector should still own event acceptance semantics: return 202 only after the event is durably appended to the replicated log.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed API edge with WAF, quota, request logs, and regional routing removes much of the custom reverse-proxy and edge rate-limit code.

technologyChoices.concern: Durable event log and ingestion stream
tradeoff: A replayable log fits billions of immutable events, multiple consumer groups, lag monitoring, and batch recovery from a bug.
A queue is simpler for discrete retries and DLQs, but long replay and partition-local stream processing are weaker.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream with retention, replication, lag metrics, and autoscaling integration removes most broker operations, partition reassignment, and disk-management work.

technologyChoices.concern: Event schemas, compatibility checks, and quarantine
tradeoff: Strict schema validation protects downstream jobs from poison messages, but event producers still need a compatibility story for older SDKs.
Keeping rejected events searchable makes replay and advertiser support practical without polluting the accepted-event log.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed schema registry and DLQ/quarantine path remove the need for a custom schema-version table, ad hoc poison-message topics, and one-off replay scripts.

technologyChoices.concern: Immutable raw event lake and replay storage
tradeoff: Object storage is the right source of truth for months or years of raw events: cheap, durable, partitionable, and independent from the hot log retention window.
The cost is operational discipline around partition layout, compaction, deletion keys, and table/catalog evolution.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed data lake with a catalog and access policy layer removes most hand-maintained file inventory, retention sweep, and raw-event discovery logic.

technologyChoices.concern: Stream processing, event-time windows, and watermarks
tradeoff: Flink-style event-time processing is strong for watermarks, keyed state, idempotent sinks, and large attribution windows.
Spark streaming can share code with batch recompute, but micro-batch latency and state handling must still meet the seconds-fresh dashboard target.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream processor with checkpoints and state backends removes much of the custom offset, timer, watermark, and restart bookkeeping.

tradeoff: In-memory state gives fast set-if-not-exists and click lookup, but persistence and failover decide how much dedup/attribution accuracy survives a restart.
Wide-column or managed KV stores handle multi-day click-state retention better, at the cost of higher per-lookup latency and explicit hot-key design.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A store with conditional writes, TTL, and durable replication removes custom cleanup jobs and most hand-rolled dedup lock tables.

technologyChoices.concern: Batch recompute, backfill, and correction jobs
tradeoff: Batch jobs trade freshness for determinism: they can reread raw events, apply fixed code, re-attribute conversions, and publish a new settled version.
Using the same engine for stream and batch reduces semantic drift, but operational isolation matters so a backfill cannot starve the live pipeline.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed batch platform with autoscaling workers, catalog integration, and job history removes most custom cluster-provisioning and backfill-run bookkeeping.

technologyChoices.concern: OLAP metrics store and pre-aggregated rollups
tradeoff: Columnar OLAP stores make campaign/time/dimension rollups cheap to scan and aggregate, but they need explicit dimension allowlists and idempotent write keys.
Search engines can serve exploratory slices and text filters, but billing metrics should still come from versioned aggregate rows.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed OLAP warehouse with partitioning, compression, and query governance removes much of the shard-management and aggregate-cache plumbing.

technologyChoices.concern: Dashboard query API, result cache, and tenant throttling
tradeoff: Caching settled windows and enforcing dimension/query allowlists protects the OLAP store from dashboard fanout and tenant abuse.
The cache must key on advertiser, metric, dimension set, window, source, version, and settlement state so provisional data never masquerades as final.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed API and cache services remove most custom per-tenant throttling, CDN invalidation, and cache-node failover work.

tradeoff: A scheduler is enough for periodic recompute, but settlement needs durable run metadata, approvals, retries, and a clear marker that tells serving which version is authoritative.
Workflow engines make retries and compensation explicit, but the metric rows and invoice references still need immutable version keys.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed orchestrator with durable history, schedules, and event triggers removes custom cron coordinators, lock tables, and manual settlement runbooks.

technologyChoices.concern: Observability, audit logging, and data quality SLOs
tradeoff: Throughput alone is not enough: accepted-event loss, consumer lag, watermark delay, duplicate-drop rate, late-event rate, attribution match rate, and batch-settlement freshness need explicit SLOs.
Audit logs should preserve who changed schemas, replayed events, approved corrections, or published a settlement version.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed telemetry with metrics, traces, logs, alerting, and retention controls removes much of the custom dashboard and pipeline-health scraping code.

patterns.name: Durable event log (Kafka)
patterns.what: Append events to a partitioned, replayable log that decouples ingestion from processing.
patterns.whenToUse: High-throughput ingestion where producers and consumers scale independently.

patterns.name: Stream processing (speed layer)
patterns.what: Consume the log continuously to produce low-latency, approximate metrics.

patterns.name: Batch reprocessing (batch layer)
patterns.what: Recompute metrics from immutable raw events for accuracy and bug fixes.
patterns.whenToUse: When billing-grade correctness and replayability are required.

patterns.name: Idempotent dedup
patterns.what: Use an event id + a seen-set/Bloom filter so retries don't over-count.
patterns.whenToUse: At-least-once delivery where counts must be correct.

patterns.name: Windowed attribution
patterns.what: Join clicks to later conversions within a time window to credit campaigns.
patterns.whenToUse: Conversion/ROI metrics that span multiple events.

patterns.name: Pre-aggregation / rollups
patterns.what: Materialize metrics by time bucket and dimension so queries are cheap.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Increment a Counter Row Per Event (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. When an event arrives, the collector synchronously does an UPDATE that increments a counter row in the metrics store (clicks++ for this campaign), and dashboards read those rows directly.

This is correct at low volume and exposes three problems the rest of the design exists to solve. First, a synchronous increment per event can't keep up with ~1M events/sec — and the load isn't even: one popular campaign's row can take tens of thousands of increments/sec — a single hot row that read replicas can't help, because the contention is on writes. The hot counter rows become a write bottleneck and ingestion stalls or drops events the moment processing falls behind. Second, ingestion is at-least-once: a retried or double-fired event increments the counter twice, so billed clicks are over-counted with no way to tell. Third, once a number is added into a counter the raw event is gone, so a counting bug can never be reprocessed out of history.

So the baseline motivates everything that follows: accept events into a durable, replayable log first, count asynchronously with idempotent dedup, and keep the raw events so a batch layer can recompute accurate, billing-grade numbers.

view.caption: The ad client emits each event to the edge collector, which increments a counter row in the OLAP store that the dashboard API queries.

steps.decisionPrompt: If each event is a synchronous counter increment, what happens at 1M events/sec, and how do you bill correctly when an event is delivered twice?
whyNow: Establishing the synchronous-counter baseline first makes it obvious why ingestion must be decoupled and durable, why counting needs dedup, and why raw events must be kept — every later step removes a specific pain from here.

recap.after: Each event synchronously increments a counter row that dashboards read directly.
recap.newRisk: Synchronous counting can't absorb the firehose, retries over-count billed events, and a counting bug can't be reprocessed.

traps.trap: Counting events with a synchronous per-event counter increment.
traps.why: It bottlenecks at the firehose, double-counts at-least-once retries, and discards the raw events needed to fix history.
traps.instead: Append events to a durable log, count asynchronously with idempotent dedup, and keep raw events for batch recompute.

steps.title: 2. Durable High-Throughput Ingestion
description: A lightweight edge collector validates and enriches each event and appends it to a durable, partitioned event log (Kafka-style). The collector does almost no work so it can absorb ~1M events/sec, batching and compressing appends for throughput.

Acceptance has a precise boundary: an event is 'accepted' only once it is replicated to the log (acked by a quorum of replicas), not merely received by the collector. The collector returns 202 only after that ack, so a billed event is durable before the client stops retrying. Producers/collectors carry a stable event_id so a producer retry after a lost ack is deduplicated downstream rather than counted twice.

The log decouples producers from consumers: ingestion never blocks on downstream processing, and many consumers can read independently at their own pace. Partition by a key (e.g. campaign id) for parallelism and ordering within a partition — but large campaigns hot-key a single partition, so salt the key (campaign_id + bucket) or repartition for aggregation, keeping ordering only where it's actually needed.

Validate schema at the edge and route invalid, malformed, or schema-incompatible events to a DLQ/quarantine rather than dropping them, so they can be fixed and replayed. Rate-limit per advertiser/source to contain abusive or runaway producers.
Also tee the raw events into an immutable data lake — the system of record that makes later reprocessing possible.

view.caption: The client's events hit the edge collector, which appends valid events to the Kafka log (archived raw to the data lake) and routes invalid/schema-incompatible events to a DLQ.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why put a log between the collector and the processors instead of processing events inline, and when exactly is an event 'accepted'?

concepts.definition: An append-only, partitioned, replicated, replayable stream of events.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decouples ingestion from processing and enables replay — the backbone of the pipeline.
concepts.example: Kafka topic partitioned by salted campaign_id, retained for days, consumed by stream + archived to a lake.

whyNow: The log is the architectural backbone; everything (real-time, batch, replay) hangs off durable, decoupled ingestion.

recap.after: Events durably accepted (replicated to the log) and buffered, decoupled from processing; invalid events quarantined.
recap.newRisk: Raw events aren't metrics yet — need processing, and counts must handle duplicates.

traps.trap: Doing aggregation synchronously in the collector.
traps.why: Ties ingestion throughput/availability to processing; a slow consumer drops events.
traps.instead: Accept fast to a durable log; process asynchronously.

traps.trap: Acking the client before the event is replicated to the log.
traps.why: A collector crash after the ack loses an accepted, billable event — durability is a lie.
traps.instead: Return 202 only after a quorum ack; rely on the stable event_id to dedup producer retries.

traps.trap: Storing raw identifiers and ignoring consent at ingest.
traps.why: Ad events carry PII-adjacent identity; raw events live for months for billing audit. Storing un-minimized data, or joining identity without consent, breaks privacy law and downstream erasure obligations.
traps.instead: Minimize at the edge: pseudonymize/hash user ids, drop fields the advertiser may not measure, and stamp a consent flag. Attribution only joins identity when ad_measurement consent is present; non-consented events still count aggregate impressions/clicks but fall back to unattributed. Partition the lake by a deletion key so erasure requests are a partition rewrite, not a full scan.

steps.title: 3. Speed Layer: Real-Time Metrics
description: A stream processor consumes the log continuously and maintains windowed aggregates (clicks/impressions per campaign per minute), writing them to the OLAP store within seconds.

Aggregate by event time (when it happened on the client), not processing time (when we saw it) — clients retry and buffer, so events arrive out of order. A watermark tracks how far event time has advanced; a window stays open until the watermark passes its end plus an allowed-lateness grace, then emits. Events later than that are routed to correction/quarantine rather than silently dropped or folded into the wrong window. Introducing event time, watermarks, and a late-arrival policy here is what makes dedup, attribution windows, and 'closed' batch windows coherent downstream.
This is the speed layer: fast and fresh, but explicitly approximate — it may miss late-arriving events or contain duplicates not yet reconciled; batch is the authority.

Use tumbling/sliding time windows keyed by campaign+dimensions; write to OLAP as incremental idempotent upserts keyed by (metric_key, window, source, version), not blind increments, so a re-emitted window doesn't double-count.

view.caption: The stream processor consumes the Kafka log and writes real-time aggregates into the OLAP store the dashboard API queries; events later than the allowed-lateness grace are routed to the DLQ/correction path instead of folded into the wrong window.

steps.decisionPrompt: What does the speed layer get wrong that you'll need to correct later?

concepts.definition: Grouping streaming events into time windows to compute running metrics.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns an unbounded stream into bounded, queryable rollups.
concepts.example: Per-minute click counts per campaign, updated as events arrive.

concepts.definition: Aggregate by when an event happened (event time), and track progress with a watermark that says 'event time has advanced past T'.
concepts.whyItMatters: Out-of-order, retried events would otherwise land in the wrong window; the watermark decides when a window can close and what counts as 'late'.
concepts.example: A click stamped 10:00:03 that arrives at 10:00:09 still lands in the 10:00 window; one arriving past the allowed-lateness grace is sent to correction.

whyNow: Real-time freshness is a hard requirement; the stream layer delivers it, while explicitly being the approximate half of the design.

recap.newRisk: Retries/duplicates over-count; late events are missed — correctness needs more.

steps.title: 4. Deduplicate for Correct Counts
description: Ingestion is at-least-once: network retries and client re-fires mean the same event can appear multiple times. Counting naively over-counts clicks — and clicks are billed.

Give each event a stable event id. The stream processor checks a dedup store (a RocksDB-backed keyed seen-set, or a Bloom filter for the approximate variant) and ignores ids it has already counted. Be precise about the guarantee: this is exactly-once within the bounded dedup window, not global exactness. End-to-end correctness needs more than the dedup store — it also depends on the processor committing the log offset, the dedup state, and the OLAP write together so a crash can't double-count.

Crash recovery: keyed state and consumed offsets are checkpointed together; on restart the processor resumes from the last checkpoint and replays the log forward. Because OLAP writes are idempotent upserts keyed by (metric_key, window, source, version), replaying the same events after a crash re-applies the same rows instead of incrementing again — so 'count once' survives a failure, not just a happy path.

Bound the dedup window (you can't remember every id forever) — its size sets the keyed-state cost (distinct ids in the window x ~50-100B/entry, tens of GB at this scale). The batch layer provides the exact, full-history dedup as the backstop for duplicates older than the window.

view.caption: Before aggregating, the stream processor checks each event id against the dedup store, then writes counts to the OLAP store.

options.name: Keyed exact dedup store (RocksDB/KV) (default)
options.description: The stream processor keeps a RocksDB-backed keyed set of seen event_ids per window and checks each event against it before counting.
pros: Exact within the window: a seen event_id is never counted twice, so billed click counts are right.
RocksDB-backed keyed state checkpoints with the stream processor, so state survives a crash and resumes from the log offset.
Per-key TTL bounds the set to the dedup window instead of remembering every id forever.
cons: State grows with distinct event ids per window — large windows mean large, expensive keyed state.
A lookup-per-event adds latency and I/O to the hot counting path.
Only exact within the bounded window; longer-horizon duplicates still need the batch backstop.

view.caption: The stream processor looks up each event id in a keyed dedup store to drop exact duplicates before writing to the OLAP store.

options.name: Probabilistic dedup (Bloom filter)
options.description: Each event_id is tested against an in-memory Bloom filter; ids that pass as unseen are counted and added, with the filter rotated per window.
pros: Tiny fixed memory footprint — billions of ids fit in a Bloom filter that stays in RAM.
Constant-time membership check keeps the per-event counting path fast.
Good enough when a small over-drop is cheaper than perfect real-time counts.
cons: False positives drop a genuinely new click, under-counting billable events.
Cannot delete ids, so it must be rebuilt/rotated per window to bound error.
You trade a known over-count (duplicates) for an unknown under-count (false positives) — risky for billing.

view.caption: The stream processor asks a Bloom filter whether an event id was probably seen before writing aggregates to the OLAP store.

options.name: No real-time dedup; rely on batch only
options.description: The speed layer counts every arriving event with no dedup lookup, leaving duplicate removal entirely to the batch layer over raw events.
pros: Simplest speed layer: count every event, no dedup store or lookup at all.
Lowest real-time latency and no extra keyed state to checkpoint.
Batch already does exact, full-history dedup, so final billed numbers are still correct.
cons: Real-time numbers are inflated by every retry/duplicate until batch corrects them.
Dashboards show provisional over-counts that visibly shrink later — confusing for advertisers.
Pushes all correctness onto the batch window; nothing is trustworthy in real time.

view.caption: The stream processor consumes the log and writes aggregates straight to the OLAP store with no dedup lookup in the path.

steps.decisionPrompt: A click event is delivered twice. How do you make sure the advertiser is billed once?

concepts.definition: Skip events whose id has already been processed, within a bounded window.
concepts.whyItMatters: Converts at-least-once delivery into exactly-once counts within the dedup window; combined with idempotent OLAP writes and checkpointed offsets, it survives crashes. Global exactness is the batch layer's job.
concepts.example: Checkpointed keyed seen-set of event ids per window; a repeat id is dropped, and a crash-replay re-upserts the same rows rather than re-incrementing.

whyNow: Correct counts are non-negotiable for billing; dedup is the mechanism and the at-least-once reality is the reason.

recap.after: Exactly-once counts within the dedup window, crash-safe via checkpointed state + idempotent OLAP writes.
recap.newRisk: Window-bounded, not global; conversions span events — need attribution and the batch exact backstop.

traps.trap: Assuming the message bus gives exactly-once for free.
traps.why: Most pipelines are at-least-once at the edges; producers retry.
traps.instead: Make counting idempotent on a stable event id; reconcile exactly in batch.

traps.trap: Treating dedup as the only thing that protects billed counts.
traps.why: Dedup removes accidental retries, but bots, click spam, and click farms are distinct events with valid ids — they pass dedup and inflate billed clicks. Invalid traffic can also be confirmed after a window already settled.
traps.instead: Add an invalid-traffic filter alongside dedup (rate/velocity per device/IP, suspicious patterns, known-bot lists): flag-and-exclude in the speed layer, re-score in batch, and emit a post-settlement invalid-traffic adjustment (a negative correction version) advertisers can see, rather than silently editing a settled number.

flows.name: Effectively-once counting via dedup
flows.note: The stream processor checks the dedup store by event id; a first-seen event is counted, a duplicate is dropped so billed clicks aren't inflated.

steps.title: 5. Attribution: Credit Clicks for Conversions
description: Advertisers care about conversions (a purchase), which happen later than and separately from the click. Attribution joins a conversion back to the click/campaign that drove it, within an attribution window (e.g. 7 days).

Define the matching key and identity up front: a conversion is matched to recent clicks by user/session/click id, and only when consent permits ad measurement. On the hot path the attribution service holds recent clicks in a keyed, time-windowed click-state store and matches an incoming conversion in near real time — it does not do a per-conversion lookup into the raw object store, which is a batch/backfill path, not seconds-latency serving.

Size the state deliberately: holding 7 days of clicks is the dominant hot-path footprint — ~1-1.7B clicks/day x 7d x ~100-200B/entry ≈ ~1-2 TB, far larger than the dedup window's tens of GB. It is sharded by match key, TTL'd at the window edge, and spilled to RocksDB rather than kept purely in RAM. Shorten the window and you shrink state but miss more late conversions (which batch then recovers); lengthen it and the hot path gets expensive.

Define the model explicitly (last-click, first-click, multi-touch) — it changes who gets credit and the numbers advertisers see. Write the result as an explicit credited_conversion record (not an implied OLAP increment) so re-attribution is idempotent.

Handle the edge cases: no matching click (record as unattributed), multiple candidate clicks (the model breaks the tie), a conversion later than the in-memory window (batch re-attribution corrects it), an attribution-model change (re-run over raw, don't mutate live state), and consent-limited identity (fall back to unattributed).

view.caption: The stream processor calls the attribution service, which matches each conversion against recent clicks in the keyed click-state store, then writes credited results to OLAP. Raw-store reads are reserved for batch re-attribution.

options.name: Stateful streaming windowed join (default)
options.description: The attribution service holds recent clicks in a keyed, time-windowed click-state store and matches each incoming conversion to its click in near real time — no per-conversion raw-store lookup on the hot path.
pros: Near-real-time: a conversion is credited to its click within seconds, not the next batch run.
Keyed, time-windowed click state matches conversions to recent clicks with an in-process/local lookup, not a data-lake scan.
Dashboards show fresh ROI/conversion numbers for open windows.
cons: Must hold every click in keyed state for the whole attribution window (e.g. 7 days) — large, costly state.
Late conversions past the in-memory window are missed and need batch correction anyway.
Changing the attribution model means rebuilding live state, not just re-querying.

view.caption: The attribution service performs a windowed click-to-conversion join against recent-click keyed state, while the stream writes credited aggregates to OLAP.

options.name: Batch-only attribution over raw events
options.description: Scheduled batch jobs join conversions to clicks by scanning the immutable raw event store, with no click state held in the stream layer.
pros: No multi-day keyed state in the stream layer — joins run over the immutable raw store.
Handles arbitrarily long attribution windows and late events the speed layer can't hold.
Re-running with a different model (last-click vs multi-touch) is just another batch job.
cons: Attribution numbers lag by the batch cadence — no real-time conversion credit.
Reprocesses large raw history each run, which is compute-heavy.
Advertisers see no live ROI feedback during a campaign, only after batch settles.

view.caption: A batch attribution job replays clicks and conversions from the raw event store, performs the windowed join, and writes credited results to OLAP.

steps.decisionPrompt: A user clicks an ad Monday and buys Thursday. How do you credit the campaign, and what's the state cost?

concepts.definition: Joining a conversion to a prior click within a bounded time window.
concepts.whyItMatters: Produces ROI/conversion metrics that span multiple events and days.
concepts.example: Last-click within 7 days credits the most recent click's campaign.

whyNow: Attribution is the ad-specific depth that separates this from a generic counter; the windowed join and model choice are the senior signals.

recap.after: Conversions attributed to campaigns within a window.
recap.newRisk: Long windows + corrections exceed what the speed layer can hold accurately — batch must reconcile.

steps.title: 6. Batch Layer: Accurate, Reprocessable Metrics
description: Periodically reprocess the immutable raw events end-to-end to produce billing-grade metrics: full dedup, complete attribution windows, and late-arriving events all included.

Batch results overwrite the speed layer's approximate numbers for the same windows (lambda-architecture reconciliation): real-time numbers are provisional, batch numbers are authoritative. 'Overwrite' is precise: batch writes metric rows keyed by (metric_key, window, source=batch, version), validates them (totals within sanity bounds vs the prior version), and only then publishes a settlement marker for the window. The query layer reads the marker to switch a window from speed to batch — it never mixes stale and fresh rows mid-flight.

Size the run: a full day is ~10-15 TB compressed (~34 TB raw) over ~86B events, so a daily reprocess must finish well inside the next day (single-digit hours on a scaled cluster) to keep settlement caught up; a multi-day backfill after a bug fix can run far longer and must not block the nightly close. Isolate replays from live serving: a backfill writes new versioned rows (or a shadow table) on a separate compute pool and is promoted atomically by bumping the settled version, so a long recompute can't corrupt the numbers advertisers are currently reading, and the nightly settlement job keeps running alongside it.

Because the raw events are immutable and replayable, a processing bug is fixed by re-running batch over history — no data loss, just recompute — and the audit trail (which version produced which billed number) is preserved.

view.caption: The batch processor replays the raw event store to recompute corrected aggregates, writes them as a new batch version in the OLAP store, and settles the window the dashboard API serves.

options.name: Lambda: separate batch + speed layers (default)
options.description: A streaming speed layer serves fresh provisional metrics while a separate batch layer recomputes the same metrics from raw events, writes a higher batch version, and publishes a settlement marker that supersedes the speed rows.
pros: Speed layer gives fresh provisional numbers; batch writes a billing-grade version and settles the window.
A processing bug is fixed by re-running batch over immutable raw events — no data lost.
Each layer is tuned independently: stream for latency, batch for completeness.
cons: Two codebases compute the same metrics, so logic can drift between speed and batch.
Serving must reconcile two sources and hide the seam where the settled batch version supersedes speed.
Operational overhead of running and monitoring both a streaming and a batch stack.

view.caption: The batch processor reprocesses the raw store, writes a new versioned batch rollup in OLAP, and settles the window, running alongside the speed layer that serves the dashboard API.

options.name: Kappa: single replayable stream layer
options.description: One stream-processing codebase computes all metrics; corrections come from replaying the retained event log through the updated job rather than a batch system.
pros: One processing codebase — corrections come from replaying the log, not a second batch system.
Fixing a bug means reprocessing from a retained log offset with the updated job.
Less operational surface: no separate batch cluster to schedule and monitor.
cons: Requires retaining the full event log long enough to replay billing history — large, costly storage.
Reprocessing months of events through the stream engine can be slower than a tuned batch job.
Very long attribution windows still strain streaming state; the simplicity can be theoretical at this scale.

view.caption: A single stream processor consumes the replayable Kafka log and writes all aggregates to OLAP, which the dashboard API queries — no separate batch layer.

steps.decisionPrompt: You discover the click counter double-counted a class of events for a week. How do you fix history?

concepts.definition: Pair a fast approximate stream layer with an accurate batch (or replay) layer that corrects it.
concepts.whyItMatters: Delivers both freshness and correctness, and makes bugs recoverable.
concepts.example: Once a day is complete, batch writes a higher-versioned rollup and publishes a settlement marker that supersedes the speed rows.

whyNow: The accuracy + reprocessability requirements demand a batch/replay layer; framing speed-vs-batch is the core architectural tradeoff of the case.

recap.after: Authoritative batch metrics; history is recomputable.
recap.newRisk: Two sources for the same metric — queries must know which to trust; serving must hide the seam.

traps.trap: Mutating settled numbers in place when a recompute changes a billed figure.
traps.why: Advertisers were already invoiced from a specific version. Silently overwriting it destroys the audit trail and surprises billing — they can't see what changed or why.
traps.instead: Keep every version immutable and append corrections as a new version. Record which metric version produced each invoice, who approved a correction, and surface the delta (old → new, reason) to the advertiser rather than letting the number quietly drift.

failureDrills.scenario: The stream processor crashes and loses in-memory windows.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It resumes from the log offset (checkpointed state); batch later corrects any gap.
failureDrills.mitigation: Replayable log + checkpointed stream state + authoritative batch recompute.

flows.name: Batch recompute overwrites provisional metrics
flows.note: Batch replays immutable raw events for a closed window with full dedup and attribution, then overwrites the speed layer's approximate numbers.

messages.label: replay raw events for closed day

steps.title: 7. Serve: Pre-Aggregation and Query
description: Dashboards can't scan billions of raw events per query. Store pre-aggregated rollups by time bucket and dimension in an OLAP/columnar store; queries read materialized aggregates and are fast.

Make the merge deterministic with the row-key/source/version model: each rollup row is keyed by (metric_key, window, source, version) and the per-window settlement marker tells the query API which source to trust. For settled windows it returns the batch rows (authoritative); for the current open window it returns speed rows (provisional) — and it stamps each bucket with a source/settled/computed_at freshness marker so the UI can show 'finalizing' vs 'final' instead of silently contradicting itself when numbers settle.

Bound cost and cardinality: pre-aggregate only an allowlist of dimensions; reject or down-sample queries that slice by un-allowlisted high-cardinality dimensions, falling back to a slower raw/columnar scan with a row/time limit. Roll up at multiple granularities (minute -> hour -> day) and enforce per-query limits (max range, max group-by cardinality) so one advertiser's broad slice can't melt the serving tier.

view.caption: The advertiser dashboard queries the serving API, which reads pre-aggregated metrics from the OLAP store fed by both the stream and batch processors.

steps.decisionPrompt: How does the query API give a user fresh numbers now and exact numbers later without contradicting itself?

concepts.definition: Materializing metrics by window+dimension ahead of query time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns an O(events) scan into an O(buckets) lookup.
concepts.example: Per-hour campaign rollups answer a month-long query from ~720 rows.

whyNow: Serving ties it together: it's where the freshness/accuracy split becomes a single coherent answer for the user.

recap.before: Metrics computed but not efficiently queryable.
recap.after: Fast dashboards over rollups; provisional-then-exact merge.
recap.newRisk: Rollup cardinality explosion across many dimensions — bounded by limiting pre-computed slices.

bottlenecks.issue: High-cardinality dimension combinations blow up rollup storage.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Pre-compute only an allowlist of slices; compute rare ones on demand from raw with row/time limits.

bottlenecks.issue: A hot partition (huge campaign) lags the stream.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Salt the key / repartition / scale consumers; the log absorbs the backlog while consumer lag is monitored.

bottlenecks.issue: Operators can't see when numbers are wrong or stale.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Monitor consumer lag, accepted-vs-rejected (DLQ) rate, duplicate rate, watermark lag, batch-correction delta (batch vs speed), OLAP ingest lag, and per-query freshness; alert on each.

flows.name: Provisional-then-exact query merge
flows.note: The query API serves authoritative batch numbers for closed windows and provisional speed-layer numbers for the still-open window, hiding the seam.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Lambda Analytics Pipeline
finalDesign.description: A thin collector accepts events fast into a durable partitioned log — an event is 'accepted' only once it is replicated to the log, not when the collector receives it — and rejects invalid/schema-incompatible events to a DLQ. The log is consumed by a stream processor (idempotent dedup via checkpointed keyed state, windowed aggregation, and near-real-time attribution against a recent-click state store -> OLAP) and archived raw to a data lake. Metric rows are written idempotently keyed by (metric_key, window, source, version); the batch layer periodically reprocesses the immutable raw events for exact, fully-attributed, billing-grade metrics that version-overwrite the provisional speed rows, then publishes a settlement marker per closed window. A query API picks batch rows for settled windows and speed rows for the open window, surfacing a freshness/source marker. Replayable raw events make any bug recoverable by recompute, with backfills isolated from live serving. Billing-domain controls are first-class, not afterthoughts: an invalid-traffic filter runs alongside dedup (with post-settlement adjustments as new correction versions), consent and PII-minimization at the edge gate whether identity can be attributed and how long raw events are retained, every billed number traces to an immutable metric version with an audit/correction trail, and the operations posture monitors consumer lag, DLQ/duplicate/watermark-lag rates, batch-vs-speed correction delta, and per-query freshness.

view.caption: The ad client emits events through the collector and Kafka log into both the speed (stream + dedup + attribution over recent-click state) and batch layers, which write the OLAP store that the dashboard API serves; invalid events at the edge and too-late events in the stream go to a DLQ/quarantine.

functional.how: Batch reprocess of raw events overwrites provisional numbers.
Windowed click->conversion join over recent-click keyed state (hot path); batch re-attribution over raw for long windows/corrections.
Pre-aggregated OLAP rollups via query API.

nonFunctional.how: Accepted only when quorum-replicated to the log; immutable data lake; invalid events quarantined to a DLQ, not dropped.
Idempotent dedup + idempotent OLAP upserts (metric_key, window, source, version) with checkpointed offsets in stream; exact, version-overwritten dedup in batch.
Pre-aggregation; queries read rollups not raw.
Immutable raw events replayed through batch.

say: Confirm both real-time freshness AND billing-grade accuracy are required.
Pin ~1M events/sec, durability, and correct (dedup'd) counts.
Establish attribution and reprocessability as first-class.
Thin collector into a durable partitioned log; archive raw.
Stream layer for real-time windowed metrics.
Dedup with event ids for correct counts.
Windowed attribution and the model choice.
Lambda reconciliation; pre-aggregation and the provisional-then-exact serving merge.
Tradeoffs: speed vs accuracy, dedup window size, rollup cardinality.
Mention kappa (replay-only) as a simpler alternative to lambda.

expectations: Ingests to a log and aggregates.
May miss dedup, attribution, and accuracy correction.
Reasons about exactly-once vs at-least-once precisely.
Discusses rollup cardinality, hot partitions, and serving-seam consistency.

followUps: Lambda vs kappa (replay-only) architecture — when would you drop the batch layer?
How do you handle very late-arriving events (days late) past the watermark and dedup window?
How would you support exactly-once end to end, and what does it cost?
How do you bound rollup storage as advertisers slice by many dimensions?
How would you detect and handle invalid traffic / click fraud (bots, click spam, suspicious IP/device patterns) as a scoped pipeline branch?
How do you evolve the event schema and replay quarantined events after a parser fix without double-counting?
What privacy controls apply — PII minimization, user-id hashing, consent filtering, retention windows, and deletion/erasure handling — and how do they shape raw-event storage and query access?
How do you support a billing dispute: audit trail of which metric version produced a billed number, and a correction workflow after recompute?
